title: PMP Formulas
summary: The formula bank in scan-friendly blocks: earned value, forecasting, three-point estimates, risk and selection, schedule float and communication channels, plus procurement. Each formula sits next to its meaning and its exam cue. Aligned to PMBOK 8, where earned value is one measurement tool inside the Finance performance domain - but every formula here is still examinable.

sections.heading: Interpretation rules first
sections.body: PMBOK 8 is outcome-based: earned value is one measurement tool among several inside the Finance performance domain, not the whole of cost control. But the numbers below still appear on the exam, so keep them sharp. Learn the sign and ratio conventions once and every formula reads the same way.

blocks.title: Read every result the same way
lines: Variance is a difference (EV minus the other measure); negative = bad.
Index is a ratio (EV over the other measure); below 1.0 = bad.
EV comes first in every earned value formula.
CV and CPI use AC; SV and SPI use PV.

blocks.title: EV is the hinge
lines: EV = percent complete x BAC.
Always the VALUE earned, never the money spent.
Get PV, EV and AC straight at one point in time and the rest follows.

sections.heading: The three measures and how they relate
sections.body: Every earned value calculation is built from three dollar figures measured at the same point in time, against the total Budget at Completion (BAC).

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 190' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><line x1='40' y1='20' x2='40' y2='160' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='40' y1='160' x2='304' y2='160' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='210' y1='28' x2='210' y2='160' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='3 3'/><text x='210' y='175' fill='currentColor' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>time now</text><path d='M44,156 C112,150 168,92 300,44' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='1.6' stroke-dasharray='5 4'/><path d='M44,156 C100,150 162,120 210,106' stroke='#2F6F5E' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M44,156 C100,147 158,108 210,90' stroke='#B5462F' stroke-width='2'/><text x='276' y='44' fill='#1F5FA8' font-size='10'>PV</text><text x='216' y='108' fill='#2F6F5E' font-size='10'>EV</text><text x='216' y='90' fill='#B5462F' font-size='10'>AC</text></svg>
figure.caption: PV planned, EV earned, AC actual. Here EV sits below PV (behind) and AC above EV (over budget).

items.text: Planned Value - the budgeted cost of the work that should be done by now.
Earned Value - the budgeted cost of the work actually done. EV = percent complete x BAC.
Actual Cost - what has actually been spent to do that work.
Budget at Completion - the total approved budget for the work.

lines: Cost variance.
Negative = over budget.
Schedule variance.
Negative = behind schedule.
Cost efficiency; value per dollar spent.
Below 1.0 = over budget.
Schedule efficiency.
Below 1.0 = behind schedule.

blocks.title: Reading the pair
lines: CV and CPI answer the cost question.
SV and SPI answer the schedule question.
A negative variance and an index below 1.0 point at the same problem.

sections.heading: Forecasting to completion
sections.body: Forecasts project today's performance forward to the end. Pick the EAC formula that matches the assumption the question states.

blocks.title: Estimate at Completion (EAC) - choose by assumption

lines: Use when current variances are TYPICAL and will continue.
The default EAC on most questions.
Use when the overrun was a ONE-OFF (atypical).
Remaining work will run at the planned rate.
Use when BOTH cost and schedule performance will persist.
Weights the remaining work by both indices.
Estimate to Complete.
What is left to spend from today.
Variance at Completion.
Negative = projected overrun at the end.
Efficiency still needed to hit the original BAC.
Use BAC on the bottom when the target has not changed.
Efficiency still needed to hit a new EAC target.
Use EAC on the bottom once the budget is revised.

blocks.title: TCPI warning
lines: TCPI above 1.0 means you must work MORE efficiently than planned to still make the target.
That is usually a warning sign, not a comfort.

sections.heading: Three-point (PERT) estimates
sections.body: Three-point estimating replaces a single guess with optimistic (O), most likely (M) and pessimistic (P) values. A wider O-to-P spread means a larger standard deviation and more risk.

lines: Triangular (simple average).
No extra weight on the most likely value.
Beta / PERT; the classic PMP formula.
The 4x weight on Most Likely makes it a weighted average.
Standard deviation of the estimate.
Optimistic and pessimistic set the range.
Variance is the standard deviation squared.
Grows fast as the O-to-P spread widens.

blocks.title: Triangular vs beta
lines: Beta = (O + 4M + P) / 6, with the 4 on Most Likely.
Triangular just divides by 3 with no weighting.
If the question stresses the most likely case, it wants beta.

sections.heading: EMV and project selection
sections.body: Money-weighted risk and the numbers used to choose between projects.

lines: Expected monetary value, in money.
Threats are negative, opportunities positive.

blocks.title: Using EMV
lines: Sum the EMV of identified risks to size contingency reserve.
Decision trees compare the EMV of each branch and pick the best.
Keep the sign: a threat lowers the branch value, an opportunity raises it.
Above 1.0 = benefits exceed cost.
Below 1.0 = costs exceed benefits.
Higher NPV is better; higher IRR is better.
Shorter payback is better.

sections.heading: Float and communication channels
sections.body: Two quick calculations that show up often - one on the network diagram, one on stakeholder communication.

lines: Also equals LF - EF.
Critical-path activities have zero total float.
n = number of people.
Paths grow fast; adding one person adds n new channels.

blocks.title: Channels worked example
lines: 10 people = 10 x 9 / 2 = 45 channels.
If a question adds people, compute before and after and take the difference.
Watch whether the question counts the project manager inside n.

sections.heading: Point of Total Assumption
sections.body: One contract figure that occasionally appears, tied to a fixed-price incentive-fee (FPIF) contract.

items.formula: PTA = ((Ceiling Price - Target Price) / Buyer share) + Target Cost
lines: Point of total assumption.
The cost above which the seller bears all further overrun.

blocks.title: When PTA applies
lines: Only fixed-price incentive-fee (FPIF) contracts have a PTA.
Buyer share is the buyer's share ratio of the overrun.
Above the PTA, every extra dollar of cost is the seller's.

source: PM Cram study notes - reworded for the current ECO-based PMP exam and aligned to PMBOK 8 performance domains.

pathMembership.purpose: Use this for calculation and chart questions.
